data.title: Texas Pacific Land: Premium Justified, Buy This Ideal Royalty Play
data.description:
Thesis

Texas Pacific Land (NYSE:TPL), for me, is like a hidden gem of Wall Street, that does not attract the spotlight like Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) but supports the very heart of American oil and gas production, the Permian Basin.

And it does this in the smartest way, without drilling itself and without billions in CAPEX, simply by collecting royalties from land it has owned for decades.

If one looks at the stock's performance, TPL is among the few names that in a decade have delivered explosive returns.

It is not cheap, of course, although, as we will see below, it has improved valuation considerably. The market pays it a premium compared to other royalty companies, but as I see it, this premium is fully justified. Because we are talking about an organization that works like a cash machine with net profit margins rarely seen in a listed company.

The asset-light model of TPL is what makes the difference. When oil and natural gas prices rise, its revenues swell without expenses rising in the same way. When prices fall, yes, TPL is affected, but to a much lesser degree than traditional producers, who carry heavy costs of drilling, equipment, and staff. That is why TPL can boast EBITDA margins above 85%.

In essence, we are talking about a landlord of the Permian, that leases land to oil and gas producers, collects royalties, and at the same time builds value in new activities such as water and pipelines.

In my previous coverage of Texas Pacific Land, I highlighted its exceptional fundamentals, noting unmatched profitability, zero debt, and strong cash flow growth. At that time, despite the stock trading near record highs, the subsequent 35% correction presented a compelling re-entry opportunity, supported by resilient revenues across both Land and Resource Management and Water Services.

My conclusion was that TPL's defensive model and dominant land position in the Permian Basin offered long-term upside potential. The current follow-up is driven by renewed momentum, robust water segment growth, and valuation levels that again suggest favorable entry points for disciplined investors. Even though its price was higher than current levels, I still believe it's an investment where it's hard to lose money, given the company's very high intrinsic value.

Below I will analyze why the Company is the ultimate defensive stock in the energy space, with a long term horizon and the reasons that I believe a "BUY" recommendation is justified.

Royalty Machine

The core of the Company's value is the production royalties. It owns more than 880,000 acres of land in West Texas, with a huge concentration in the Delaware Basin, which is the most productive part of the Permian.

This means that no matter how the balance in the industry shifts, TPL always has ready rent from every barrel that comes out of the subsurface of its land.

To put it simply, if Exxon, Chevron or Conoco produces 1 million barrels from acreage that belongs to TPL, it gets its share. And this without having to risk with drilling or exploration.

It is like being the owner of a property and being paid every month by your tenant, only here we are talking about energy production worth billions.

The numbers clearly show the strength of this model. The Company has managed in recent years to consistently present rising revenue from royalties, following the increase of production in the Permian.

Even when oil corrects, the operators of the area, big names with strong balance sheets, continue pumping, because they have some of the lowest break-even costs globally. This ensures that TPL will keep collecting.

This is its biggest cushion. Investors know that as long as the Permian remains the number one oil production area in the US, TPL will be at the heart of the equation, profiting from every drop that is pumped. And because we are talking about the Permian, which has reserves for decades, the story here is long-term and strong.

Surface & Pipeline Assets

TPL is not only royalties. The second big part is the use of its land on the surface. This includes leases for pipelines, for midstream infrastructure, even for telecommunications infrastructure. Think of it as a real estate play inside the energy industry.

Companies that want to run pipelines, set up infrastructure, or build facilities on acreage owned by TPL pay for usage rights. This creates a stable recurring income that does not depend directly on oil and gas prices.

Most investors look only at the royalties, but the value of the surface can prove to be gold in the future, especially if new infrastructure develops in the Permian. From natural gas pipelines to data centers and renewable installations, TPL is sitting on huge land of opportunities.

Already the revenue from surface leases has grown on average in recent years, and in my opinion, it is the most defensive part of the business model. Because even if oil falls, the infrastructure remains and the leases keep running.

Water Business

Here is the real bet. Water in the Permian is perhaps more valuable than oil itself, because without water, drilling cannot happen.

TPL has developed a fully integrated activity in water management, from supplying fresh water to recycling produced water and disposing of brine. The water business, I would say, is the catalyst that can change the outlook of the stock. It already brings in hundreds of millions in revenue, but more important is that it has huge growth potential.

As production in the Permian rises, so do water needs. And TPL has the advantage of owning rights and infrastructure exactly where the game takes place.

The big investment in the Orla Desalination Project shows its strategy, to enter strongly in water processing, not only for oil needs, but also for other industries. If we think of the future with data centers, AI, batteries, and green energy, water will be a critical asset.

The Company can play a role in this. It seems to me, that the market today underestimates this segment. It sees only royalties. Yet over time, the water business can become the second main revenue pillar, with margins that will resemble classic royalties.

Financials & Valuation

Looking at the numbers, Texas Pacific Land resembles more a fund than an energy company. Operating profit margins (EBITDA margin) remain steadily above 85%, while the net profit margin exceeds 70%. In other words, from every dollar that enters the cash register, almost 70 cents stay clean. This is rarely seen in a listed company, especially in an energy stock.

The company carries no meaningful debt. The balance sheet is clean, with cash exceeding immediate obligations and with zero leverage. This gives TPL huge flexibility, because it does not depend on banks or bond markets to finance its activities. On the contrary, it has the luxury to finance everything from its own cash flows.

On the level of distributions, dividends are poor, but it mainly invests in buybacks. This is very important, because the free float is already low, and every repurchase increases the value per share. In my opinion, buybacks at TPL matter much more than the dividend, because they work as a built in multiplier in the stock's performance, as they artificially raise the value of the shares left to investors.

Now, if we speak of valuation, the picture is a bit more tricky. The stock trades at a P/E above 46x, while the sector is not above 14. On an EV/EBITDA basis, TPL trades around 34x+, while the sector is close to 7x. At first sight, it looks expensive.

However, here the premium is justified. Because this is not a cyclical oil company that tomorrow could sink under CAPEX or debt. We are talking about a landlord that collects regardless of who drills and who owns the rigs. Risk is lower, cash flows are more predictable, and the long term value of land in the Permian keeps rising.

In addition, TPL has growth drivers that other royalty companies do not. Water is the most typical example. In 2024, 2025 water services revenue grew more than 25% annually, and the trend shows it can become a key pillar. If one values this business as a separate midstream company, then the P/E premium of TPL becomes more logical.

Personally, I think the market still reads TPL as a simple royalty play, while it is something more; it is an energy real estate company with embedded growth in water. This means its multiple can stay at premium for many years, without being considered excessive.

Stock Overview

The stock's behavior on the board has a dual interpretation.

In the short term, it has been in a multi-month downward trend, and cumulatively it has lost 49% from its all-time highs reached at the end of 2024.

Although it looks like the stock is collapsing, a deeper analysis shows that such periods of cumulative losses have happened before. For example, Nov 2022 to Jun 2023 the stock had lost 54%; from Jun 2021 to Jan 2022, it had lost 47%; and in the second half of 2019, it had lost 68%, partly due to the pandemic.

And each time, shareholders came back stronger, delivering correspondingly large returns of +319%, +189%, and +501%, always at new all time highs. In the long term, therefore, the trend is upward.

No one can say with certainty where, and if, the current downward move will stop, nor if the stock will repeat such behavior and continue to new all-time highs with outperformance.

I will play this scenario, considering both the above analysis and the fact that at these levels the stock again gives a technical buy signal, setting the upward trend for at least a re-approach of the previous highs at $1650, with first resistance at $1160-$1170, which means a target around +80%. Even if I am not sure I will divest, even at that point.

Risks

The main risk is concentration in the Permian. If for any reason activity there decreases, TPL will be directly affected.

Also, oil and gas prices are always an unpredictable factor.

Moreover, there are regulatory and ESG risks. Pressures for reduced drilling and for green transition can affect the sector.

From my perspective, these are long term challenges that do not change the core story of TPL. The Permian will keep producing for decades, and TPL will keep collecting.

A more immediate risk is the volatility of the stock. Because the Company has low liquidity, with limited free float, it can have intense fluctuations.

Conclusion

Overall, Texas Pacific Land is one of the most unique and attractive placements in the energy space.

It is not just a royalty company. It is a landlord that sits on gold, while at the same time building new pillars of value in water and surface.

The stock is not cheap, but it is premium with merit. For anyone who thinks long term and wants exposure to energy in the cleanest way, TPL is an ideal choice.

It is a stock that is not played as a trade but as a core holding in a portfolio that seeks stability and, at the same time, growth.

For me, it is the epitome of the Ultimate Royalty Play. And that is why I consider it a must have for anyone who believes the Permian will continue to be the beating heart of American energy for many more years.

data.title: United Natural Foods: Solid 2025 Results, But Not The Bargain It Once Was
data.description:
When I last wrote about United Natural Foods, Inc. (NYSE:UNFI) back in December 2022, I argued that the company was undervalued despite the risks. At the time, UNFI had just started FY 2023 with upward sales growth, an increasing customer base, but also with heavy debt and very thin margins. I was cautiously optimistic that the company would improve its efficiency and also reduce its leverage. An interesting stock for patient investors. Since my previous article, the stock price has fluctuated but has been on the rise for the last year, rewarding investors by 89.76%.

Fast forward almost three years, United Natural Foods released its FY 2025 annual earnings, and the company has improved its top line consistently, it generated strong free cash flow, and it reduced a significant amount of debt. However, now that the stock price has risen so much, the valuation appears less attractive. Therefore, at current levels, I see the stock as a wait and hold for a better entry point.

Company Overview

United Natural Foods is the fourth largest distributor of natural, organic, and specialty foods in North America. It has over 30,000 customers and it generates revenue across three segments, namely Natural, Conventional, and Retail, as seen in the table below.

You probably have not bought directly from them, but if you have ever walked into a Whole Foods Market or smaller health-focused grocery store, there is a good chance United Natural Foods was behind the supply chain that stocked the shelves.

One of the biggest recent events for the company was the acquisition of SuperValu for $2.9 billion in 2018. That acquisition expanded its reach but also negatively impacted its leverage and reduced its margins. Since then, the challenge has been to financially benefit from the deal and decrease the debt intake. One of the most important customer relationships remains with Whole Foods, owned by Amazon, which accounts for a significant portion of sales. This relationship is also contractual until 2032, giving an indication of the stability of this revenue. Furthermore, due to strong execution, the company has increased the objectives it put in place, as seen below.

Growth Drivers

Looking at the next financial year, there are a few things that look to benefit the company. One of the growth drivers is the healthy market it is in. The demand for healthy foods is still growing, and that directly benefits United Natural Foods. The global organic food and beverage market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 13.9% to reach $564.22 billion by 2030.

Another strength that stands out to me is that the company has also been improving its operations by investing in automation and e-commerce. This can help keep costs down for the company and improve margins in the long run. Another positive is that it has started to bring down its debt. With lower interest costs, it now has more flexibility to reinvest in the business. The challenge will be to turn these improvements and growth potential into earnings and profit. Over the last two financial years, the company has delivered annual losses.

Financial Updates

United Natural Foods' revenue has steadily grown over the last seven financial years to $31.78 billion in FY2025. That is more than triple what its topline was ten years ago. A big driver was, of course, the costly 2018 SuperValu acquisition, rather than organic growth. What is important for investors to be aware of, while the acquisition added scale, it has negatively impacted previously higher margins. More recently, inflation and higher food prices have played a role in growing sales, but at the same time, this has increased costs, so we have not seen much improvement to gross profit.

In FY 2025, there was a bigger loss of $118 million compared to a $112 million loss in FY2024. This is because of costs like fuel, wages, and technology upgrades that went up faster than the company could raise its prices. For a food distributor that makes only small profits on each sale, even small cost increases can negatively impact the bottom line.

In FY 2025, we can see that there was $369.8 million in positive levered free cash flow, compared to only $33 million the year before. This improvement was because the company cut back on debt and paid less interest. This has allowed for an increase in cash after its expenses. This is a positive sign as it indicates that the business has cash to invest back into the business and continue to pay its debts.

In the balance sheet, we can see that there is still a lot of debt at $3.45 billion, compared to just $45 million in cash. The good news is that it’s been paying this down and generating more cash, which helps keep things under control. For investors, it means the company can manage for now, but debt is still something to watch closely.

Valuation

As in my previous article, I have compared United Natural Foods to some of its larger peers, namely Sysco Corporation (SYY) and US Foods Holding Corp. (USFD). In the Quant relative peer valuation below, we can see that United Natural Foods still looks quite cheap on a number of measures, like its price-to-sales and price-to-cash-flow ratios. What this means is that investors are paying less for each dollar of sales the company generates compared to Sysco and US Foods. However, its forward price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of 24.78 is higher than its historical average and higher than that of US Foods. While the company is improving, the current P/E suggests that much of the positive progress may already be priced in. We can also see that the other companies had positive earnings TTM, which was not the case for United Natural Foods. Combined with its low margins and high debt, for me, this indicates that the company is more of a hold with potential than an obvious buy at the moment.

Risks

As I noted in my earlier article, this is a business characterized by low margins, high debt, and heavy dependence on a small number of large customers. None of those risks has gone away. Margins are still low, meaning even a small increase in costs can cut through profitability. Debt has come down, but it is still high enough to be cautious of. The company is also very reliant on a few larger customers, such as Whole Foods. This lack of diversity is also a risk to consider. An example of this happened a few months ago when a cyberattack impacting the two partners disrupted operations and negatively impacted sales potential.

Final Thoughts

When I last wrote about United Natural Foods in 2022, the company offered long-term potential at a discounted price despite some risks. Since then, management has made real progress. We have seen sales grow, cash flow is stronger, and debt has come down. Operationally, the company is in better shape today, but the stock rally means much of that progress is already reflected in the price. Therefore, I see the stock as a hold with potential. The business is steadily improving, but it now carries less upside unless its earnings continue to improve.

data.title: MP Materials: A Small, Long-Term Position Is In Order
data.description:
2025 has been quite the year so far for rare earths producer MP Materials (NYSE:MP) with its 340% YTD uptick triggered by the Trump administration's disruptive trade policy that ended up in a series of fortunate events for the company.

That the stock has been far more positively impacted than the sector as such is evident from the fact that its rise far exceeds that for the VanEck Rare Earth and Strategic Metals ETF (REMX), which has seen a robust 68% increase of its own.

As is usually the case with massive gains in small time periods, though, the stock's market valuations are now completely out of whack. For this reason, here I make a case for why the stock is a no-go for investors with the short- to medium-term time horizon in mind. But for long term investors, which, for the purpose of Long Term Tips is defined as at least five years, MP is still one to buy. There are risks, of course, which are highlighted as well.

Why has MP Materials exploded?

Before jumping into the valuations, first, let's look at why MP has exploded in the first place because this has implications for what can happen next. The stock prices trends in 2025 can be divided into two parts. The first is between January-July 10 and the second is July 10 onwards. The details are below:

Tariff challenges double MP in H1 2025

In H1 2025, the stock had already doubled. With the Trump administration's trade policy significantly increasing tariffs, rare earths came under challenge for two reasons. One, some 80% of US demand for them was met through imports and two, China, which was significantly impacted by these measures, was a substantial exporter of rare earths (see graphic below).

As China retaliated with requirements of export licenses, essentially making the process potentially more cumbersome at the very least, the supply of rare earths was at risk. This was a challenge since they have applications across industries, including the military.

Enter MP Materials, which produces over 10% of the world's rare earths and defines itself as America's "..only fully integrated rare earth producer with capabilities spanning the entire supply chain—from mining and processing to advanced metallization and magnet manufacturing". It's no wonder it suddenly looked more attractive.

DoD investment and Apple partnership

While China later eased the restrictions, the writing on the wall was clear. The US needed to secure supplies. The government took a big step in this direction with the help of the Defense Production Act, which was enabled by an executive order in March 2025. This allowed the government to enter into public-private partnerships.

The scene was now set for MP Materials as an investment target. By July 10, in what the company calls a "transformational" partnership, the DoD had bought preferred shares worth USD 400 million in the company, which translates into a 15% stake, making it the single biggest shareholder for the company. This investment, which will go towards building the magnet manufacturing 10x facility among other goals, led to over 50% jump in a single trading session on July 10.

USD 500 million Apple contract

That's hardly the end of the story, though. The DoD investment was followed by a USD 500 million long-term partnership with Apple (AAPL) to supply recycled rare earth magnets. Towards this end, along with the goals of the government investment, the company is now looking to expand its Fort Worth magnetics facility. The news led to another 20% jump in the stock in a single trading session

Q2 results reflect robust revenue growth and contracting losses

As the company released its Q2 2025 results on August 7, the stock received further support as the price rose by ~5% each in the two following trading days. It can be inferred here that the results were positive, what with the record production of NdPr and revenues surge by 84% YoY.

The company also managed to narrow its net loss by 24% YoY. While the loss is disappointing on the face of it, the fact that it's driven by its big capital investments in manufacturing offers comfort. These investments resulted in a 74% increase in operating expenses between 2022 and 2024 and also an operating loss.

Valuations are stretched for now...

With so much impetus for a stock price rise, MP's valuations have risen beyond any proximity of being reasonable. Consider this. Its TTM P/B ratio is at 11.1x compared to 2.33x for REMX at the end of August.

Next, as per consensus analyst estimates on Seeking Alpha, MP Materials will become profitable once again next year. But even then, its forward P/E is rather high 79.7x compared with REMX, which is at 40x right now. While REMX's forward ratio isn't immediately available, it can be safely assumed that for next year, it's likely to be even lower. This makes the potential gap between MP's and REMX's valuations even starker.

...but relax over the longer term

However, a few years down the line, MP's valuations are expected to cool down significantly. By 2029, that the stock's forward P/E cools down to below REMX's present levels, to 28.91x. And to even more palatable levels of 14.1x by 2030.

Since it's likely that REMX's levels would be even lower by then, I also compared MP with Energy Fuels (UUUU), the US based pure play uranium miner that acquired significant rare earth assets after purchasing Australia's Base Resources late last year. While its forward P/E for 2026 at 125.3x is even bigger than MP's, by 2029, it reduces to just 9.72x.

This raises the question - is there a Buy case for MP then? I'd say yes. Because even by 2029, its revenue will still be 1.4x that for Energy Fuels, maintaining its lead in rare earths production. And the lead would be even bigger than that suggested by the revenues, as Energy Fuels is also a uranium miner, which could well account for a significant part of its production, depending on the market conditions. Moreover, MP Materials has a history of rare earth production, while Energy Fuels has just got into it. And also MP has a history of profits, while UUUU is just getting started.

The risks

Despite my bullishness, however, there are risks to consider too. For one, by the time the long-term target period of 2030 is reached, another election would have taken place in the US. And that means a possible change in policy.

Even under the Trump administration, a de-escalation of the tariff war can't be ruled out, as evident from the fact that China has already relaxed on rare earth exports. If rare earth supply from China becomes abundant once again, domestic producers like MP Materials can lose their current edge.

Next, besides Energy Fuels, the heating up of rare earths could also result in more competition for MP Materials. Niron Magnetics being a case in point. The startup, with support from automobile majors like Stellantis (STLA) and General Motors (GM), is expected to become operational in 2027.

Investor takeaway

All things considered, there's still a Buy case for MP. But with all the risks involved, one that won't hurt the portfolio significantly. The global policy landscape is in a flux. And since that's the key driving force behind MP Materials' stock price right now, any change to it, can impact the stock going forward.

That said, there's no denying the significance of rare earths and the company's important position in supplying them in the US. Also, with US-China challenges being a long-standing reality of these times, the need for security of critical supplies can be expected to sustain. I'm going with a Buy rating, but only for long-term investors.

data.title: Rolls-Royce Aerospace Stock Doubled Again, But I Still See More Upside Ahead
data.description:
Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF, OTCPK:RYCEY, OTC:RLLCF) easily has been one of my favorite aerospace and defense investing ideas. Since my last report, the stock price has doubled, easily outperforming the S&P 500, and since its initiated the Rolls-Royce has shown a massive 430% surge. With stock price returns as strong as these, we have to be mindful about overvaluation. That is why I will be analyzing the stock, including the H1 2025 earnings, and updating my price target in this report.

The Bullish Thesis For Rolls-Royce And Associated Risks

Rolls-Royce is one of the companies that has used the pandemic to cut costs and focus on cost-discipline. Before that, it was a company with below average margins. What helps the company is that it has exposure to several markets, all of which are currently seeing positive growth cycles:

  • Civil Aviation
  • Defense
  • Power Systems

In Civil Aviation, the company has increasing flight hours as a tailwind in combination with rising production on wide body airplane programs such as the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350. In Defense, expanding budgets offer opportunities for propulsion systems sales or military aircraft, naval vessels, and submarines. For both of these markets, the delivery of the propulsion system is not necessarily big. The company, however, makes up for that through highly lucrative aftermarket services revenues. This offers a multi-decade revenue tail per engine, and so the fact that Rolls-Royce has been renegotiating these aftermarket deals or TotalCare agreements is a significant driver of financial growth. In the energy market, the company benefits from high demand for energy infrastructure partially driven by data center power demand, and nuclear energy hooks right into the data center angle, but broadly in the energy transition, nuclear power is also more prominently featured compared to several years ago. In that regard, the company is positioned well with its small modular reactor, or SRM. The Airbus A350's Trent 1000 XWB engine has been a success story as well, and Rolls-Royce is combining all lessons learned into the UltraFan. The UltraFan is scalable next generation aero engine which could be used on iterations of the Airbus A350 and Boeing 787, and a derivative of the engine may also be suitable for a 200-220 seat airplane.

While the stock price almost suggest otherwise, the sky is not the limit and Rolls Royce has been facing pressures as well. That is part of the story on why their margins have been low for quite some time. The engine sales come at relatively low margins, that also means that there is little margin for error, even more so given that when engines are relatively new there is not a lot of services required. Rolls-Royce ran into durability issues on the Trent 1000 Package C and TEN engines with premature component wear, corrosion, and cracking. It led to dozens of Boeing 787 airplanes being grounded between 2016 and 2020 and triggered redesign and customer compensations. On the Boeing 787, operators can choose between engines from GE Aviation and Rolls-Royce. So, the problems have not done anything good for the company's share on the Boeing 787 program. The Trent 7000 used on the Airbus A330neo is based on the same engine core as the Trent 1000, and that also has given rise to durability concerns for that engine. The A350 engine has performed relatively well in terms of durability, but also on that engine we saw earlier than expected shop visits. However, these issues can be more categorised as teething issues rather than actual flaws. It may seem like Rolls-Royce can bring an engine to the market that comes with the promised durability. The reality is that while this does hurt the financials and the reputation, this is an issue that many aero engines have faced.

Time-on-wing and efficiency are the core strengths of an engine, and that is where aero engine manufacturers have had some difficulties. The market requires more efficient engines, and by the way the thermodynamics and governing efficiency drivers work, that also means that the hot section of the engine has to become hotter, and that in turn means that advanced materials and manufacturing is required. If for whatever reason, there are problems, the reliability and efficiency of the engine reduce, and that affects the profitability of the operator. Since manufacturers sell engines with certain specifications on durability and efficiency, those costs rather easily are passed on to Rolls-Royce in case anything goes wrong.

That has been a risk for Rolls-Royce as it has been for any manufacturer. Going forward, that risk is somewhat limited given that the company already encountered these issues and the associated costs. On commercial side, that leaves mostly the risk of delays in production ramp up for the airplane programs. The risks on the nuclear side of the business is that while demand for new nuclear reactors is high, there are several designs and manufacturers offering their solutions to the market. So, the clear risk is having a promising solution but not being selected to deliver on bids.

Civil Aerospace And Energy Drive H1 2025 Earnings

Rolls-Royce reports detailed financial results every six months. For H1, we see that revenues grew 13% to £9.06 billion, with gross profits increasing 33% to £2.57 billion and operating profit surged 50% to £1.73 billion. This brought the operating margin to 19.1% up from 14% in the same period last year. Those margins are simply impressive and substantially better than what we see among aerospace peers.

Civil Aerospace sales grew 17% to £4.79 billion on higher engine deliveries, including spare engines, higher long-term services agreements revenues and materials sales. Services sales increased 19% while OEM sales grew 12%. Gross profit increased 50% to £1.48 billion, while operating profit increased 63% to £1.2 billion bringing the margins from 18% to 24.9%. Margins improved driven by the profitable aftermarket sales and net contract margins have also been improving as time-on-wing improved. So, we see the clear connection between time-on-wing and margins. We do note by engine type, large engines drove the growth, while business aviation was up 1%, regional was down 12% and V2500 sales were down 3%. The IAE V2500 engine is used on the Airbus A321ceo and that also means that over time, if more and more A321ceos are phased out the V2500 revenues for Rolls-Royce will also decline.

While defense offers a growth opportunity, we see little of that for Rolls-Royce at this point. Revenues rose 1% to £2.2 billion, gross profit declined 2% to £462 million, and operating profit remained stable at £342 million. Margins of 15.4% were comparable to last year. The absence of substantial growth in the defense results was driven by one-off submarine contract in H1 2024 and supply chain constraints, which apparently remain a bottleneck in some areas.

Power Systems sales grew 20% to £2 billion, while operating profit grew 89% to £313 million, driving a strong margin increase from 10.3% to 15.3%. Strong data center demand for power generation drove a substantial part of the revenue growth, but we also note that there was 19% growth in Governmental sales and 6% growth in Marine sales. These three growth areas offset an 18% decline in the industrial end market.

Rolls-Royce Is Outperforming Its Mid-Term Targets

The strong results in civil aerospace and power systems provided enough confidence for Rolls-Royce to increase its full year guidance. Operating profit guidance has been lifted from £2.7-£2.9 billion to £3.1-£3.2 billion, while free cash flow guidance improved from £2.7-2.9 billion to £3-£3.1 billion. So, we see a strong improvement. We do note that free cash flow conversion has been lowered somewhat but overall I consider this a very bullish guidance lift that also provides more confidence in the company's ability to reach the mid-term targets. In fact, it seems that those targets have been lifted. Previously, the mid-term targets for operating profit were £2.5-£2.8 billion and that has been lifted by £1.1 billion. For free cash flow the company's mid-term target is now £4.2-£4.5 billion compared to £2.8-£3.1 billion earlier. So, essentially, the company has reached its mid-term target, meaning FY27 targets two years earlier than expected.

With that in mind, it is not extremely surprising that we saw the stock perform as strong as it did.

Rolls-Royce Stock Has Additional Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously had a $11.14 price target for 2026, and that price target has increased substantially to $18.69, driven by a significant improvement in the earnings and free cash flow outlook in combination with a higher EV/EBITDA multiple for the aerospace and defense sector. Rolls-Royce stock is definitely not cheap and contrary to my prior assessment, we note that the company is now trading ahead of the peer group. So, chances of the stock price doubling again in a short period of time is unlikely. However, we do note that the company has appreciable margins with additional growth ahead, and it is returning cash to shareholders through repurchases and dividends while being in a net cash position. The company currently has a £1 billion share repurchase program in place until the end of the year and I expect that the company will fully utilize the repurchase authorization. Beyond that, the company has space to repurchase at least £3 billion in shares through 2027. So, I remain bullish on Rolls-Royce. I don't think we will see substantial increases in the stock price like we saw before, but there definitely is additional upside ahead.

Conclusion: Rolls-Royce Is On An Impressive Trajectory

The financial performance and outlook in future years has easily exceeded my expectations, and the improved outlook not just for 2025 but for the years beyond has been a key driver of upside for the stock price. At the same time, we note that upsides in the range of 50%, as I saw before, are no longer there, but that is because the stock price also rose sharply. I remain positive on the outlook for Rolls-Royce and I believe that defense has yet to fully deliver on the growth prospects. I also note that beyond the global commercial aerospace strength, Rolls-Royce could see substantial tailwinds from European defense and energy demand.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Planet Labs Surges On Satellite Growth, But Multiples Are Tough To Justify
data.description:
Back in June, I rated Planet Labs PBC (NYSE:PL) a buy after finally crossing the positive operating cash flow barrier. It’s an encouraging sign for a growing company, but I didn’t imagine the stock would perform like it has, more than doubling in the last few months.

This is obviously great news for the people who bought Planet Labs back then, and the surge is based largely on a growing alternative business of selling and servicing their satellites instead of merely being a provider of their own satellite data. It’s almost a whole new company with a new business model, and the announcement of a new Berlin factory excited many.

At the same time, this changes how we value the company, and likely means existing estimates, which were offered on the old model of selling content rather than physical satellites, are likely far off. In particular, once the new factory ramps up, we can likely expect revenue to be much higher, as buying a space-borne satellite is obviously more expensive than buying a few photos.

So we’re going to be looking at the company through fresh eyes, both having a look at somewhat tepid Q2 earnings, and trying to come to terms with new multiples that are, frankly, rather high.

Operating Cash Flow Remains Strong, But Losses Persist

While Q1 was very encouraging on a number of metrics, including Planet Labs’ first positive operating cash flow and a roughly break-even non-GAAP EPS, things looked a little bit weaker in Q2. They beat on revenue, to be sure, but the non-GAAP EPS was just a penny ahead of estimates, and still a loss. For the first half, that gives us:

(Source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

After narrow beats in both quarters this year, what we have for a statement of operations is good but not great. Revenue is rising, losses are falling, but on the whole they’re still a hair below break-even even on a non-GAAP basis. The estimates suggest that’s going to continue at least through FY2027, though again the growth of the satellite sale business is going to throw a lot of those old estimates out the window. The silver lining is that the company is actually pretty solidly operating cash flow positive now.

($30 thousand)
